cards.definition: The target soil pH for coffee cultivation, at which applied fertilizer is used most efficiently by the plant.
cards.analogy: The 'room temperature' setting for nutrient uptake — soil chemistry works best in a specific band, not just 'less acidic is always better.'
cards.why_it_matters: Fertilizer applied to soil far from this pH is partly wasted, regardless of how correct the dose itself is.
cards.cloze: The soil pH should be maintained at around ___ in coffee soils for best fertilizer-use efficiency.

cards.definition: How often coffee soil should be tested at minimum, to keep fertilizer and lime recommendations current.
cards.analogy: A regular health check-up, not a one-time diagnosis assumed to hold forever.
cards.why_it_matters: A farm that hasn't tested soil in this window is applying fertilizer somewhat blind, however experienced the grower.
cards.cloze: Coffee soils should be tested compulsorily once every ___ years.

cards.definition: The recommended window in the year for applying lime to coffee soil, timed to the dry season rather than the monsoon.
cards.analogy: A dry-season task, not a monsoon one — lime applied into heavy rain is prone to washing away before it can act.
cards.why_it_matters: Lime applied outside this window is more likely wasted input than a genuine soil correction.
cards.cloze: The ideal period for lime application in coffee zones is ___.

cards.definition: The threshold above which a recommended lime dose is too large to apply in one go, and must instead be split across two years.
cards.analogy: A dose large enough that it needs to be taken in two installments rather than all at once, the way a large loan gets structured in tranches.
cards.why_it_matters: A farm applying its full lime recommendation in a single season, above this threshold, is likely over-shocking the soil.
cards.cloze: If the recommended lime dose exceeds ___ metric tonnes per hectare, it must be split into two equal applications over two years.

cards.definition: The block size fields should be divided into for soil sampling, so results reflect real variation across a farm rather than one average.
cards.analogy: Testing several rooms of a house separately instead of assuming one reading represents the whole building.
cards.why_it_matters: A single soil sample for a large, varied estate would mask real block-to-block differences this sampling size is designed to catch.
cards.cloze: For soil testing, fields should be divided into blocks of ___ hectares each for separate sampling.

cards.definition: The size of the hole dug to collect a representative soil sample for testing.
cards.analogy: A sample scoop sized to reach the depth where coffee roots actually feed, not just a surface scrape.
cards.why_it_matters: A sample taken too shallow risks missing the nutrient zone that actually matters for the crop.
cards.cloze: Soil samples for testing should be collected from a hole dug to about ___ inches in diameter and depth.
